exercise.title: Interactive TNA Exercise
exercise.subtitle: Learn Transition Network Analysis hands-on — build sequences, see networks, and discover patterns.
exercise.concept_data_body: TNA starts with sequences — ordered lists of actions, events, or states. Each row represents one person's journey. Pick an example dataset or build your own to get started.
exercise.concept_frequency: How often does each state appear?
exercise.concept_frequency_body: Before building a network, check which states dominate. The frequency chart shows total counts, while the distribution plot shows how states spread across timesteps.
exercise.concept_transitions_body: A transition matrix counts how often state A is followed by state B. Raw counts show absolute frequency; normalized probabilities show the likelihood of each transition per row.
exercise.concept_network_body: Each state becomes a node, each transition becomes a directed edge. Edge thickness reflects transition weight. Try different model types to see how the network changes.
exercise.concept_centrality_body: Centrality measures identify important nodes. InStrength sums incoming edge weights (popular destinations). Betweenness measures how often a node lies on shortest paths between others (bridges).
exercise.concept_pruning_body: Pruning removes weak edges below a threshold, making the network easier to interpret. Compare the original and pruned networks side by side.
exercise.concept_advanced_body: Patterns find recurring subsequences (e.g., view→quiz→submit). Clustering groups similar sequences together to reveal learner archetypes.
exercise.build_your_own_desc: Enter custom sequences in a spreadsheet grid
exercise.assign_columns_desc: Tell TNA which column is the actor (who), which is the action (what), and which is the timestamp (when). TNA will group events by actor, sort by time, and use the action column as states.
exercise.counts_explain: How many times each transition was observed in the data.
exercise.probs_explain: Each row divided by its sum — shows the probability of transitioning to each state.
exercise.how_probs_body: Each cell in the probability matrix is computed by dividing the raw count by the row total. For example, if "view" was followed by "quiz" 5 times and "view" had 15 total outgoing transitions, the probability is 5/15 = 0.33. This means that 33% of the time, "view" was followed by "quiz". Each row sums to 1.0 (100%).
exercise.press_play: Press Animate to watch transitions being counted one by one.
exercise.no_model: Could not build model from current data.
exercise.pruning_live_note: The network above updates live as you move the threshold. Edges below the threshold are removed.
exercise.pick_dataset_prompt_desc: Select a sample dataset from the sidebar to begin exploring Transition Network Analysis.
exercise.pick_block_prompt_desc: Choose an analysis block from the sidebar to visualize your data.
exercise.pipe_dataset_desc: Select a sample dataset to explore. Each contains real-world-style event logs with actors, actions, and timestamps.
exercise.pipe_viewdata_desc: Inspect the raw data and assign column roles. TNA needs to know which column is the actor (who), action (what), and timestamp (when).
exercise.pipe_buildmodel_desc: Choose a model type and build the transition network. Each type captures different aspects of the sequential data.
exercise.model_relative_desc: Row-normalized transition probabilities. Each row sums to 1, showing the likelihood of transitioning to each state.
exercise.model_frequency_desc: Raw transition counts. Shows how many times each transition occurred in the data.
exercise.model_cooccurrence_desc: Co-occurrence within sequences. Captures which states tend to appear together regardless of order.
exercise.model_attention_desc: Attention-weighted transitions. Gives more weight to transitions that are distinctive to specific sequences.
sna.title: SNA Interactive Lab
sna.subtitle: Explore social networks — edge lists, centrality, communities & graph metrics
sna.intro_text: Social Network Analysis (SNA) studies the structure and patterns of relationships between actors. Choose a sample network to begin exploring.
sna.edge_list_hint: This is the weighted edge list for the selected network. Click Build Network to visualize and analyze.
sna.select_analysis_hint: Choose an analysis from the sidebar to explore the network structure.
sna.build_error: Failed to build network model. Please try a different scaling method.
sna.communities_info: Nodes in the same community are more densely connected to each other than to nodes in other communities. The network graph above is colored by community.
sna.custom_instructions: Enter comma-separated values for each column. Each position represents one edge (e.g., position 1 in From matches position 1 in To).
sna.custom_error_empty: From and To fields cannot be empty.
sna.custom_error_mismatch: From and To must have the same number of entries.
sna.custom_error_weights: Weights must have the same number of entries as From/To.
sna.guide_adjacency_text:
The adjacency matrix is the mathematical foundation of a network. Each cell (i,j) shows the weight of the connection from node i to node j. A zero means no connection. In undirected networks, the matrix is symmetric. The diagonal shows self-connections (if any).

The heatmap visualizes the matrix — darker cells indicate stronger connections. Look for patterns: clusters of dense connections may indicate groups or communities within the network.
sna.guide_centrality_text:
Centrality identifies the most important or influential nodes in a network.

Degree counts connections. In-Degree and Out-Degree count incoming and outgoing connections.

In-Strength and Out-Strength sum the weights of incoming and outgoing connections.

Betweenness measures how often a node lies on the shortest path between other nodes — high betweenness nodes are bridges.

Closeness measures how close a node is to all others — high closeness means quick access to the entire network.
sna.guide_communities_text:
Communities are groups of nodes that are more densely connected to each other than to the rest of the network.

This uses label propagation: each node starts with its own label, then iteratively adopts the most common label among its neighbors. When the process converges, nodes sharing the same label form a community.

Look for how communities relate to the network structure — do central nodes connect different communities?
sna.guide_metrics_text:
Graph-level metrics describe the overall structure of the network.

Density is the ratio of actual to possible edges — a density of 1.0 means every node is connected to every other.

Average degree is the mean number of connections per node.

Reciprocity (for directed networks) measures the proportion of mutual connections — if A connects to B, does B also connect to A?
sna.sg_metrics_p1: Graph-level metrics summarise the overall structure of a network in a single number. Rather than describing one node or one edge, they capture a property of the whole network — how connected it is, how information might spread, or how many mutual relationships exist. These are typically the first things researchers report when describing a social network.
sna.sg_metrics_p2: Think of graph metrics as the “vital signs” of a network. Just as a doctor checks pulse and temperature before diving deeper, a network analyst checks density and degree before interpreting centrality or communities.
sna.sg_metrics_density_p1: Density is the ratio of actual edges to the maximum possible number of edges. A density of 1.0 means every node is connected to every other node (a complete graph). A density of 0.0 means no connections at all. Most real social networks are sparse — density values between 0.05 and 0.25 are common.
sna.sg_metrics_density_p2: High-density networks tend to be cohesive and redundant — information spreads quickly and there are many alternative paths. Low-density networks are more efficient but fragile — removing a key node may disconnect the graph.
sna.sg_metrics_avgdeg_p1: Average degree is the mean number of connections per node. In a directed network, this is usually reported separately as average in-degree and average out-degree. A high average degree means nodes tend to be well connected; a low average degree means most nodes have few ties.
sna.sg_metrics_recip_p1: Reciprocity applies only to directed networks. It measures the proportion of mutual connections — for every edge from node A to node B, does an edge also exist from B to A? A reciprocity of 1.0 means all relationships are mutual. A reciprocity near 0 means most connections are one-directional.
sna.sg_metrics_recip_p2: In social networks, high reciprocity often indicates friendship or close collaboration, while low reciprocity may indicate hierarchical or influence-based relationships (e.g. follower/following on social media).
sna.sg_metrics_avgw_p1: In a weighted network, average weight is the mean edge weight across all connections. This provides context for interpreting centrality scores: a node with high strength in a low-average-weight network is genuinely dominant, whereas the same strength in a high-average-weight network may be less remarkable.
sna.sg_cent_p1: Centrality measures identify the most important or influential nodes in a network. “Important” can mean different things — most connected, most reachable, or sitting at the intersection of many paths. Different centrality measures capture different notions of importance, so researchers often report several.
sna.sg_cent_p2: In social networks, centrality helps answer questions like: Who is the most popular student? Who spreads information fastest? Who serves as a bridge between otherwise disconnected groups?
sna.sg_cent_degree_p1: Degree is simply the count of connections a node has. In undirected networks, every edge counts once. In directed networks, in-degree counts how many nodes point to a given node (incoming links), and out-degree counts how many nodes a given node points to (outgoing links).
sna.sg_cent_degree_p2: High in-degree nodes are popular — many others connect to them. High out-degree nodes are active initiators of connections. A node with both high in-degree and high out-degree is a central hub.
sna.sg_cent_strength_p1: In weighted networks, strength sums the edge weights rather than just counting edges. A node with three strong connections may be more influential than one with ten weak connections. In-Strength and Out-Strength are the weighted equivalents of In-Degree and Out-Degree.
sna.sg_cent_strength_p2: For example, in a co-authorship network, a researcher who collaborated extensively with many colleagues (high total co-authored papers) will have high strength even if their raw degree is similar to peers.
sna.sg_cent_between_p1: Betweenness centrality measures how often a node lies on the shortest path between other pairs of nodes. A node with high betweenness is a bridge or broker — it connects otherwise separate parts of the network. Remove such a node and the network may become disconnected or much less efficient.
sna.sg_cent_between_p2: In classrooms, high-betweenness students often bridge social groups. In collaborative networks, high-betweenness researchers connect different research communities. Betweenness is especially useful for identifying structural brokers who may not be the most popular but are critically positioned.
sna.sg_cent_close_p1: Closeness centrality measures how close a node is to all other nodes — specifically, the inverse of the average shortest path distance. A node with high closeness can reach all other nodes quickly, making it efficient for spreading information.
sna.sg_cent_close_p2: Nodes with high closeness are well-positioned to disseminate knowledge or coordinate activity. In a classroom network, a student with high closeness centrality could spread information to the whole class in fewer steps than anyone else.
sna.sg_cent_interpret_p1: No single centrality measure is universally best. Use degree/strength when you care about direct connections, betweenness when you care about brokerage and bridges, and closeness when you care about reach and efficiency. Compare measures across nodes — a node that ranks highly on all three is likely a true structural hub.
sna.sg_comm_p1: Communities (or clusters) are groups of nodes that are more densely connected to each other than to the rest of the network. In social networks, communities often correspond to friend groups, research clusters, workgroups, or shared interests — real social structures that emerge from the pattern of connections.
sna.sg_comm_p2: Community detection is unsupervised: the algorithm discovers groupings from the network structure alone, without any prior knowledge of who belongs together. The result is a partition of nodes into groups that maximises internal connectivity relative to external connectivity.
sna.sg_comm_label_p1: Label propagation is a fast, intuitive algorithm. Each node starts with its own unique label. In each iteration, every node adopts the most common label among its neighbours. When no node changes its label, the process converges and nodes sharing the same label form a community.
sna.sg_comm_label_p2: Label propagation is non-deterministic — running it twice may give different results for networks with ambiguous community structure. It works well for large networks and does not require specifying the number of communities in advance.
sna.sg_comm_mod_p1: Modularity measures the density of links inside communities compared to links between communities. Algorithms like Modularity Greedy and Girvan-Newman optimise this score. Modularity Greedy builds communities bottom-up by merging nodes that increase modularity most. Girvan-Newman works top-down by progressively removing the edge with the highest betweenness centrality.
sna.sg_comm_walk_p1: Walktrap uses random walks: the idea is that a random walk on a graph tends to get “trapped” in densely connected regions. By analysing where random walks of length 3–5 tend to stay, the algorithm identifies communities as regions where walks rarely escape.
sna.sg_comm_spectral_p1: Spectral methods use the eigenvalues of the graph Laplacian to project nodes into a lower-dimensional space where clustering becomes easier. This approach can detect communities with complex shapes that modularity methods may miss, but it is computationally heavier for large graphs.
sna.sg_comm_interpret_p1: Look for communities that correspond to meaningful social groupings. Do the nodes in each community share attributes (same role, same department, same study group)? Are there nodes that sit between communities with many cross-community connections? Those bridge nodes often have high betweenness centrality — comparing centrality and community results together is a powerful interpretive strategy.
sna.sg_adj_p1: The adjacency matrix is the mathematical foundation of any network. It is a square matrix where rows and columns both represent nodes. Each cell [i, j] contains the weight of the connection from node i to node j. A zero means no direct connection; a positive value means a connection exists with that weight.
sna.sg_adj_p2: Every network can be represented as an adjacency matrix, and every adjacency matrix defines a network. The two representations are equivalent — the matrix is just more convenient for mathematics, while the graph is more convenient for human interpretation.
sna.sg_adj_dir_p1: In an undirected network, the matrix is symmetric: cell [i,j] equals cell [j,i]. Friendships and co-authorships are often undirected — if Alice is connected to Bob, Bob is connected to Alice.
sna.sg_adj_dir_p2: In a directed network, the matrix may be asymmetric: Alice can follow Bob without Bob following Alice. The row represents the source and the column represents the destination. In directed networks you can read rows as “who does this person reach out to?” and columns as “who reaches out to this person?”
sna.sg_adj_heatmap_p1: The heatmap visualises the matrix with colour intensity proportional to edge weight — darker cells indicate stronger connections. Patterns to look for:
sna.sg_adj_heatmap_li_dark_rows: Dark rows — a node that connects strongly to many others (high out-strength)
sna.sg_adj_heatmap_li_dark_cols: Dark columns — a node that receives many strong connections (high in-strength)
sna.sg_adj_heatmap_li_blocks: Block structure — clusters of cells that are dark together, suggesting communities
sna.sg_adj_heatmap_li_diagonal: Diagonal values — self-connections (if any), indicating that a node is connected to itself
sna.sg_adj_heatmap_li_sparse: Sparse rows/columns — peripheral nodes with few or weak connections
sna.sg_adj_ops_p1: The adjacency matrix enables powerful mathematical analyses. Raising the matrix to a power reveals indirect paths: the [i,j] cell of A2 counts the number of paths of length 2 from node i to node j. The eigenvalues of the matrix reveal the largest communities and structural features. All centrality measures computed above are derived ultimately from this matrix.
sna.sg_adj_ops_p2: Understanding the adjacency matrix helps you appreciate that network visualisation is just one way to see the same underlying data structure — the matrix contains the full, lossless representation of the network.
ai_gen.title: Generate Dataset with AI
ai_gen.desc_tna: Describe the kind of event-log data you want. The AI will generate realistic sequences with actors, actions, and timestamps.
ai_gen.desc_sna: Describe the kind of social network you want. The AI will generate a realistic weighted edge list with nodes and connections.
ai_gen.placeholder_tna: e.g. 20 university students using an online learning platform over 2 weeks, with activities like reading, watching videos, taking quizzes, and posting in forums...
ai_gen.placeholder_sna: e.g. A research lab with 15 scientists who collaborate on papers. Some are senior and collaborate widely, others are newcomers with fewer connections...
ai_gen.max_attempts: Maximum attempts reached. The AI could not produce a valid dataset. Please try a different description.
ai_gen.parse_error: The generated format was invalid. You have {{remaining}} attempt(s) left. Try again or rephrase your description.
